summary: Gruber analyzes Apple's rejection of Google Voice apps from the App Store, pushing back against the popular narrative that AT&T was solely behind the decision. He argues that Google Voice threatens Apple's own business model, not just AT&T's, since carrier subsidies depend on assumptions about customer spending on voice, data, and SMS. He frames the rejection as a competitive move against Google, asking readers to consider how they'd react if Microsoft offered a similar service. The post includes two notable updates: first confirming AT&T involvement, then later citing FCC filings showing the decision was Apple's alone.
key_points: Apple rejected Google's official Google Voice app and removed existing third-party Google Voice apps from the App Store
Gruber argues Apple has its own financial incentives to block Google Voice, since carrier subsidies depend on expected customer spending on voice, data, and SMS
He reframes the issue as competitive rather than carrier-driven: Google Voice is a mobile service from the maker of a rival phone OS
The post's updates contradict each other — first confirming AT&T involvement, then citing Apple's FCC response claiming the decision was Apple's alone
money_quotes: But does anyone really think AT&T pulls the strings in this relationship?
Google Voice doesn't just interfere with the carrier's business model, it interferes with Apple's iPhone business model.
I can see why people are pissed that Apple has come out against Google Voice for iPhone, but I can't see how anyone is surprised.
Google Voice is a mobile phone service provided by the maker of one of the biggest competitors to the iPhone OS.

What if Google Voice were instead Microsoft Voice? And what if Windows Mobile were as modern and competitive as Android? Would you be as surprised then that Apple is discouraging iPhone owners from using the service? Just saying.
key_insight: Apple's rejection of Google Voice was less about AT&T pressure and more about Apple protecting its own business model and competitive position against Google as a rival platform maker.
